title: AG2 (AutoGen) Official Quickstart Guide
description: Getting started guide for AG2, the open-source successor to AutoGen. Covers installation (pip install ag2[openai]), core agent types (ConversableAgent, UserProxyAgent, GroupChatManager), LLMConfig for model configuration, and basic two-agent conversation patterns. Note: ag2 and autogen are PyPI aliases for the same package.

title: AG2 Group Chat with Research Team: Planner, Scientist, Critic, and Executor
description: Real-world AG2 example constructing a multi-agent research team with Admin, Engineer, Scientist, Planner, Critic, and Executor agents. Uses GroupChat with up to 50 rounds for collaborative research, constrained speaker transitions, and demonstrates how a Critic agent improves output quality through adversarial review.
code:
import autogen

llm_config = autogen.LLMConfig(
    config_list=[{"model": "gpt-4o", "api_key": "..."}]
)

planner = autogen.AssistantAgent(
    name="Planner",
    system_message="Suggest a research plan. After the plan is approved, say DONE.",
    llm_config=llm_config,
)

scientist = autogen.AssistantAgent(
    name="Scientist",
    system_message="Execute research steps and report findings.",
    llm_config=llm_config,
)

critic = autogen.AssistantAgent(
    name="Critic",
    system_message="Review and critique the research plan. Suggest improvements.",
    llm_config=llm_config,
)

groupchat = autogen.GroupChat(
    agents=[planner, scientist, critic],
    messages=[],
    max_round=20,
    speaker_selection_method="auto",
)
manager = autogen.GroupChatManager(groupchat=groupchat, llm_config=llm_config)

planner.initiate_chat(
    manager,
    message="Research the latest advances in quantum computing."
)

title: AG2: GroupChat Advanced Concepts
description: Deep dive into AG2 GroupChat covering speaker selection strategies (auto, round_robin, random, manual), constrained speaker transitions with allowed_or_disallowed_speaker_transitions, send_introductions for agent self-introduction, sequential chats with initiate_chats(), and custom speaker selection callables.

title: AG2: Perform Research with Multi-Agent Group Chat
description: Real-world tutorial constructing a research team with Admin, Engineer, Scientist, Planner, Critic, and Executor agents. Uses GroupChat with up to 50 rounds for collaborative research. Compares results with and without a Critic agent to demonstrate how adversarial review improves output quality.

title: Agentic Memory: A-Mem Paper (Zettelkasten-Inspired Agent Memory)
description: Research paper on A-Mem, a Zettelkasten-inspired memory architecture for LLM agents. Creates interconnected knowledge networks through dynamic note construction, link generation, and memory evolution. Achieves 85-93% token reduction vs baselines while doubling multi-hop reasoning performance on LoCoMo and DialSim datasets.

title: Agentic RAG: Agent-Controlled Retrieval Augmented Generation
description: Agentic RAG gives an autonomous agent control over retrieval: deciding when to search, what queries to run, which sources to consult, and when it has gathered enough evidence. The five-component architecture (Router, Retriever, Grader, Generator, Hallucination Checker) forms a loop, not a pipeline. Achieves 78% on complex multi-hop queries vs 34% for traditional RAG. Start with basic RAG and progress to agentic RAG only when simpler patterns fall short.

title: Anthropic Agent Design Patterns: Tool Surface, Programmatic Tool Calling, and Caching
description: Technical reference from Anthropic's skills repository covering tool surface design (when to use bash vs dedicated tools), programmatic tool calling (PTC) for composing tool calls into scripts to reduce latency and token cost, scaling with tool search and Skills for on-demand loading, context management with editing and compaction, and caching workarounds for agent contexts.

title: Anthropic Computer Use: Building Desktop Automation Agents with Claude
description: Official Claude API documentation for the computer use tool enabling agents to see screens and control desktops via screenshot capture, mouse/keyboard actions, and zoom capability. Covers tool types (computer, text_editor, bash), the agent loop pattern for multi-step tasks, coordinate scaling for high-resolution displays, prompt injection defenses with automatic classifiers, and cache control strategies for long agent loops. Computer Use 2.0 GA achieves 72.5% on OSWorld with >90% success on well-scoped production tasks.

title: Anthropic: Building Effective Agents
description: Anthropic's foundational guide on agent patterns, distinguishing workflows (predefined code paths) from agents (LLM-directed processes). Covers prompt chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, and evaluator-optimizer patterns. Emphasizes starting simple and adding complexity only when measurably beneficial.

title: Building Agents with the Claude Agent SDK
description: Practical guidance on building agents using the Claude Agent SDK covering the agent loop (gather context, take action, verify work, repeat), context gathering strategies (agentic search, semantic search, subagents, compaction), action mechanisms (tools, Bash/scripts, code generation, MCPs), and verification methods (defined rules, visual feedback, LLM-as-judge).

title: Building Governed AI Agents: Handoffs, Guardrails, and Centralized Policy Enforcement
description: OpenAI's official cookbook example combining handoffs, guardrails, tracing, and centralized policy enforcement for a private equity firm assistant. Shows how to build a PEConcierge triage agent with DealScreeningAgent and PortfolioAgent specialists, input guardrails blocking invalid queries, and the RECOMMENDED_PROMPT_PREFIX for reliable handoff prompts.
code:
from agents import Agent, Runner, handoff, InputGuardrail, GuardrailFunctionOutput
from agents.extensions.handoff_prompt import RECOMMENDED_PROMPT_PREFIX
from pydantic import BaseModel

class PEQueryCheck(BaseModel):
    is_valid: bool
    reasoning: str

guardrail_agent = Agent(
    name="PE Query Guardrail",
    instructions="Check if the user is asking a valid question for a PE firm.",
    output_type=PEQueryCheck,
)

async def pe_guardrail(ctx, agent, input_data):
    result = await Runner.run(guardrail_agent, input_data, context=ctx.context)
    final_output = result.final_output_as(PEQueryCheck)
    return GuardrailFunctionOutput(
        output_info=final_output,
        tripwire_triggered=not final_output.is_valid,
    )

deal_screening_agent = Agent(
    name="DealScreeningAgent",
    handoff_description="Handles deal sourcing, screening, and initial evaluation.",
    instructions=f"{RECOMMENDED_PROMPT_PREFIX}\nYou are a deal screening specialist.",
)

portfolio_agent = Agent(
    name="PortfolioAgent",
    handoff_description="Handles questions about existing portfolio companies.",
    instructions=f"{RECOMMENDED_PROMPT_PREFIX}\nYou are a portfolio management specialist.",
)

pe_concierge = Agent(
    name="PEConcierge",
    instructions=f"{RECOMMENDED_PROMPT_PREFIX}\nRoute each question to the right specialist.",
    handoffs=[deal_screening_agent, portfolio_agent],
    input_guardrails=[InputGuardrail(guardrail_function=pe_guardrail)],
)

title: Building Specialized AI Sub-Agents in Claude Code: Multi-Agent Orchestration
description: Practical comparison of Skills vs Sub-agents vs Agent Teams in Claude Code. Skills operate in the same context window, sub-agents get isolated context for cognitive focus, and Agent Teams coordinate as peers with shared state. Includes a decision framework: single agent for single-file fixes, sub-agents for 2-3 layer features, agent teams for API contract negotiation, and external orchestrators for 5+ parallel agents.

title: Claude Code Sub-Agents and the Orchestrator Pattern
description: Detailed guide to the orchestrator pattern with Claude Code sub-agents. Covers the four phases: clarify (identify affected projects), plan inside-out (backend to frontend), dispatch with context packets (project path, rules, task, acceptance criteria), and verify cross-project consistency (type alignment, imports, error handling). Key insight: three focused agents consistently outperform one generalist working three times as long.

title: Claude Code: Create Custom Sub-Agents
description: Official documentation for Claude Code sub-agents system. Covers built-in sub-agents (Explore, Plan, General-purpose), custom sub-agent definitions as Markdown files with YAML frontmatter in .claude/agents/, tool restrictions (whitelist/blacklist), model selection per subagent (sonnet, opus, haiku), persistent memory scopes, hooks, MCP server scoping, and experimental Agent Teams for peer-to-peer coordination.

title: CrewAI Examples: Write a Book with Flows
description: Official CrewAI example demonstrating multi-crew orchestration with Flows. An outline crew generates structure, then chapter crews process each section in parallel with accumulated context, and a final join step compiles the complete book. Illustrates @start, @listen, and @router decorators for complex workflow control.

title: CrewAI Official Documentation
description: Comprehensive documentation for CrewAI covering agents (role, goal, backstory), crews (sequential and hierarchical processes), tasks (guardrails, structured output, context dependencies), and flows (event-driven orchestration with @start and @listen decorators). Includes YAML-based configuration, checkpointing, streaming, and async execution.

title: CrewAI: Build Your First Crew
description: Step-by-step guide to scaffolding a CrewAI project using the CLI. Covers project structure (config/, tools/, crew.py, main.py), YAML agent and task definitions with {topic} placeholders, @agent/@task/@crew decorators, and running with crewai run. Demonstrates sequential process with SerperDevTool for web search.

title: CrewAI: Build Your First Flow (Event-Driven Orchestration)
description: Tutorial on CrewAI Flows, the newest orchestration pattern combining direct LLM calls, crews, and procedural code. Uses @start() and @listen() decorators for event-driven orchestration, Pydantic models for structured state management, and section-by-section processing with accumulated context. Visualize with crewai flow plot.

title: CrewAI: Build a Game-Building Agent System
description: Complete tutorial building a Python game generator using three CrewAI agents in a sequential pipeline: senior_engineer_agent writes initial code, qa_engineer_agent reviews for errors, chief_qa_engineer_agent validates. Demonstrates @CrewBase decorator, YAML configuration, context passing between tasks, and allow_delegation controls.

title: DeepLearning.AI: AI Agents in LangGraph (Andrew Ng)
description: Andrew Ng's short course covering ReAct agents, LangGraph state machines, tool use, human-in-the-loop, and multi-agent orchestration. Includes hands-on coding exercises building agents progressively from simple tool-calling to complex multi-agent systems with persistence and memory.

title: Four Single-Agent Patterns: ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, ReWOO, Reflexion
description: Comparative analysis of the four dominant single-agent patterns. ReAct loops Thought-Action-Observation; Plan-and-Execute plans all steps upfront then executes with cheaper models; ReWOO uses only 2 LLM calls via placeholder substitution; Reflexion adds self-critique retries. Includes hybrid strategies used in production.

title: From Basic Tool Calling to Advanced ReAct Agents: A Complete Implementation Guide
description: Progressive tutorial building from basic OpenAI tool calling through LangChain (@tool decorator, AgentExecutor) to advanced LangGraph workflows (StateGraph, ToolNode, MemorySaver). Concludes with a full ReAct agent implementation with multiple tools (calculator, text analyzer, random number generator, time functions) and production considerations for error handling and security.

title: Function Calling Deep Dive: Building LLM-Powered Tools and Agents
description: Comprehensive guide covering basic function calling with OpenAI (tool definitions, parameter schemas, execution dispatchers), parallel function execution with ThreadPoolExecutor and async patterns using AsyncOpenAI, function calling with Claude (Anthropic's tool use API), multi-step agent workflows with a ToolAgent class, and error handling with Pydantic schemas.

title: Google Agent Development Kit (ADK)
description: Google's open-source, code-first framework for building, evaluating, and deploying AI agents. Supports Python, TypeScript, Java, and Go with multi-agent systems, built-in tracing, MCP tool integration, and one-command deployment to Cloud Run. Agents are defined as Python modules with function tools, built-in tools (Google Search, Code Execution), third-party API tools (LangChain), and MCP tools for external data sources.

title: LangChain Long-Term Memory Documentation
description: Official LangChain documentation on long-term memory that persists across threads and sessions, unlike short-term per-thread memory. Built on LangGraph stores saving JSON documents organized by namespace and key. Supports InMemoryStore for development and PostgresStore for production. Provides structured memory types: semantic, episodic, and procedural.

title: LangGraph Human-in-the-Loop: Interrupt Patterns in Python
description: Comprehensive guide to three HITL patterns in LangGraph: binary approve/reject, approve/edit/reject, and tool-level routing where safe tools auto-approve while sensitive tools require human review. Addresses the double-execution problem and recommends focused approval nodes. Production patterns use PostgresSaver or RedisSaver instead of MemorySaver.

title: LangGraph Official: Human-in-the-Loop Quickstart
description: Official LangGraph tutorial covering both Graph API (StateGraph with nodes and conditional edges) and Functional API (@task, @entrypoint decorators) for building HITL agents. Demonstrates the newer interrupt() function for pausing execution inside nodes, with Command(resume=...) for resuming. Requires a checkpointer for state persistence.

title: LangGraph ReAct Agent: Build from Scratch
description: In-depth walkthrough building a ReAct agent using LangGraph's StateGraph from first principles. Covers state definition with MessagesState, tool creation with @tool decorator, node functions, conditional routing with should_continue, and graph compilation. Compares manual construction against the create_react_agent() shortcut function.

title: LangGraph: Build a ReAct Agent with Human-in-the-Loop Approval
description: End-to-end workflow for building a production LangGraph ReAct agent with interrupt-based human approval gates. Covers defining tools, building a StateGraph with agent and tool nodes, compiling with a checkpointer, interrupting before sensitive actions, inspecting and updating state, and resuming after human approval.
workflow: Define tools using @tool decorator (safe tools like lookup_inventory and sensitive tools like send_notification)
Build a StateGraph with agent node (LLM call) and tools node (tool execution)
Add conditional routing: if the LLM calls a tool, route to tools node; if not, end
Compile the graph with MemorySaver checkpointer and interrupt_before on sensitive action nodes
Invoke the graph with initial user input; execution pauses before the sensitive node
Inspect state with app.get_state(config) to see the pending tool call
Optionally update state with app.update_state(config, {approved: True}) to approve
Resume execution with app.stream(None, config); the agent processes the approved action and continues

title: LangMem: Memory Management for LangGraph Agents
description: LangChain's official memory layer for agents. Provides create_manage_memory_tool() and create_search_memory_tool() for in-conversation memory operations, plus a background memory manager that automatically extracts, consolidates, and updates knowledge. Native integration with LangGraph's Long-term Memory Store using InMemoryStore (dev) or AsyncPostgresStore (production).

title: MIRIX: Multi-Agent Memory System for LLM-Based Agents
description: Research paper on MIRIX, a multi-agent memory system with six specialized components: Core Memory (user facts), Episodic Memory (time-stamped events), Semantic Memory (entity relationships), Procedural Memory (task instructions), Resource Memory (documents/media), and Knowledge Vault (sensitive data). Features active retrieval and achieves 85.4% on LOCOMO with 99.9% storage reduction.

title: Mem0: Production-Ready Long-Term Memory for AI Agents
description: Open-source memory layer (56K+ GitHub stars) that dynamically extracts, consolidates, and retrieves salient information from conversations. Graph-based variant captures complex relational structures. Achieves 26% relative improvement over OpenAI's memory on LOCOMO with 91% lower p95 latency and >90% token cost savings vs full-context approaches. Evaluated against MemGPT, LangMem, Zep, and RAG baselines.

title: Microsoft AI Agents for Beginners: Tool Use Lesson
description: Lesson 4 of Microsoft's 12-lesson AI agents course covering tool use design patterns, function/tool calling fundamentals with Azure OpenAI, implementation using Microsoft Agent Framework (@tool decorator, AzureAIProjectAgentProvider), Azure AI Agent Service with built-in tools (Bing Search, File Search, Code Interpreter, Azure Functions), and security considerations (read-only database roles, sandboxed environments).

title: Model Context Protocol (MCP): Connecting Agents to External Tools and Data
description: Official MCP documentation covering the open standard for connecting AI agents to external systems. MCP provides three primitives (Tools for executable functions, Resources for contextual data, Prompts for reusable templates) over STDIO or Streamable HTTP transport. Agents connect to MCP servers via a client that discovers available tools at runtime, enabling one integration to work across Claude, ChatGPT, VS Code, Cursor, and any MCP-compatible client.

title: Multi-Agent Orchestration Patterns: Supervisor, Swarm, Hierarchical, Router, Pipeline, Consensus
description: Comprehensive comparison of six orchestration patterns with trade-off matrices. Supervisor (hub-and-spoke) for structured workflows; Hierarchical for 10+ agents; Swarm for decentralized exploration; Router for classification-based dispatch; Pipeline for sequential stages; Consensus for high-stakes decisions. Includes anti-patterns (over-orchestration, infinite loops, no cost attribution) and a decision framework.

title: OpenAI Agents SDK: Handoffs and Guardrails Example
description: Official example combining agent handoffs with input and tool guardrails. Shows how to define specialist agents with handoff_description, create a triage agent that routes to specialists, implement input guardrails (blocking math homework requests), and add tool guardrails (blocking secrets in tool calls). Demonstrates the recommended RECOMMENDED_PROMPT_PREFIX for handoff prompts.
code:
from agents import Agent, Runner, handoff, InputGuardrail, GuardrailFunctionOutput
from agents.extensions.handoff_prompt import RECOMMENDED_PROMPT_PREFIX
from pydantic import BaseModel

class MathHomeworkOutput(BaseModel):
    is_math_homework: bool
    reasoning: str

guardrail_agent = Agent(
    name="Guardrail check",
    instructions="Check if the user is asking you to do their math homework.",
    output_type=MathHomeworkOutput,
)

async def math_guardrail(ctx, agent, input_data):
    result = await Runner.run(guardrail_agent, input_data, context=ctx.context)
    return GuardrailFunctionOutput(
        output_info=result.final_output,
        tripwire_triggered=result.final_output.is_math_homework,
    )

billing_agent = Agent(name="Billing agent", handoff_description="Handles billing questions")
refund_agent = Agent(name="Refund agent", handoff_description="Handles refund requests")

triage_agent = Agent(
    name="Triage agent",
    instructions=f"{RECOMMENDED_PROMPT_PREFIX}\nRoute each question to the right specialist.",
    handoffs=[billing_agent, refund_agent],
    input_guardrails=[InputGuardrail(guardrail_function=math_guardrail)],
)

result = await Runner.run(triage_agent, "I need a refund for my order")
print(result.final_output)
print(result.last_agent.name)

title: OpenAI Agents SDK: Official Documentation
description: Official documentation for the OpenAI Agents SDK covering core primitives (agents, handoffs, guardrails), the built-in agent loop, Python-first design, function tools with automatic schema generation, MCP server tool calling, sessions for persistent memory, human-in-the-loop, tracing for debugging, and realtime/voice agent support.

title: OpenAI Agents SDK: Orchestration and Handoffs Guide
description: Official API guide on agent orchestration patterns: handoffs (agent-to-agent transfer), agents as tools (nested invocation without transfer), and the difference between them. Handoffs transfer control to the new agent; agents-as-tools return results to the calling agent. Covers recommended prompts, input filtering, and streaming handoffs.

title: OpenAI Agents SDK: Quickstart
description: Official quickstart guide for the OpenAI Agents SDK. Covers installation (pip install openai-agents), hello world with Runner.run_sync(), adding function tools with @function_tool, multi-agent handoffs with handoff_description and triage routing, and basic tracing for debugging agent execution flows.

title: Pipeline Pattern: Sequential Agent Stages for Content Generation
description: Pipeline pattern where agents are chained sequentially, each transforming and enriching data before passing to the next stage. Ideal for content generation (research, outline, draft, edit), data ETL, and compliance verification. Latency is the sum of all stages; failures cascade. Each stage should validate inputs and handle errors gracefully.
workflow: Define the sequential stages (e.g., Researcher -> Outliner -> Writer -> Editor)
Create a specialist agent for each stage with clear input/output contracts
Pass the user request to the first stage agent
Each agent processes its input and produces structured output for the next stage
Validate output at each stage before passing to the next (guardrails)
If any stage fails, retry with modified instructions or halt with a clear error
The final stage agent produces the output delivered to the user
Log all intermediate results for debugging and audit trails

title: Plan-and-Execute Pattern: Upfront Planning with Step-by-Step Execution
description: A powerful model plans all steps upfront as a JSON step list or DAG, then a cheaper model executes each step. On failure, only remaining steps are revised. Best for structured, predictable workflows like insurance claims or financial reports. More token-efficient than ReAct for predictable tasks, but rigid when reality surprises. Hybrid approach: drop into ReAct for steps that surprise.
workflow: Receive user request and pass to the Planner agent (powerful model)
Planner decomposes the task into an ordered list of steps with dependencies
Planner outputs a structured plan (JSON or DAG) specifying step order and data flow
Executor agent (cheaper model) processes each step sequentially or in parallel where possible
If a step fails, feed the error back to the Planner for re-planning of remaining steps
Completed step results are preserved; only future steps are revised
After all steps complete, synthesize results into the final output
For steps that produce unexpected results, optionally switch to ReAct mode for that step

title: ReAct Agent from Scratch with Gemini 2.5 and LangGraph
description: Step-by-step implementation of a ReAct agent using Google's Gemini 2.5 Pro/2.0 Flash with LangGraph. Includes a real-world weather tool using the Open-Meteo API, multi-turn conversation continuation, state management with MessagesState, and conditional routing. Demonstrates how to swap LLM providers without changing the agent graph structure.
code:
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, MessagesState, START, END
from langgraph.prebuilt import ToolNode, tools_condition
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from langchain_google_genai import ChatGoogleGenerativeAI

@tool
def get_weather(city: str) -> str:
    """Get current weather using Open-Meteo API."""
    import requests
    geocode_url = f"https://geocoding-api.open-meteo.com/v1/search?name={city}"
    geocode = requests.get(geocode_url).json()
    if not geocode.get("results"):
        return f"City '{city}' not found."
    lat = geocode["results"][0]["latitude"]
    lon = geocode["results"][0]["longitude"]
    weather_url = (
        f"https://api.open-meteo.com/v1/forecast"
        f"?latitude={lat}&longitude={lon}&current_weather=true"
    )
    weather = requests.get(weather_url).json()
    temp = weather["current_weather"]["temperature"]
    code = weather["current_weather"]["weathercode"]
    return f"Weather in {city}: {temp}C, weather code {code}"

tools = [get_weather]
llm = ChatGoogleGenerativeAI(model="gemini-2.0-flash").bind_tools(tools)

def agent_node(state: MessagesState):
    return {"messages": [llm.invoke(state["messages")]}

graph = StateGraph(MessagesState)
graph.add_node("agent", agent_node)
graph.add_node("tools", ToolNode(tools))
graph.add_edge(START, "agent")
graph.add_conditional_edges("agent", tools_condition)
graph.add_edge("tools", "agent")
app = graph.compile()

result = app.invoke({"messages": [("user", "What's the weather in Tokyo?")]})
print(result["messages"][-1].content)

title: ReAct Pattern - Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
description: Tutorial on the ReAct (Reason+Act) pattern where agents interleave thought, action, and observation steps in a loop. Explains why ReAct is the default agent pattern, its strengths (adaptive, grounded, debuggable), and weaknesses (linear token cost, myopic planning). Includes practical decision guidance for choosing between ReAct, Plan-and-Execute, ReWOO, and Reflexion.

title: SWE-bench: Evaluating Agents on Real-World GitHub Issues
description: SWE-bench evaluates LLM-based agents on real GitHub issues. Given a codebase and issue description, agents must generate patches evaluated against the repo's test suite. SWE-bench Verified (500 human-validated tasks) has contamination concerns. SWE-bench Pro (1,865 multi-language tasks from GPL/private code) shows Claude Opus 4.5 at 45.9% and GPT-5.2 at roughly 23%. SWE-bench Live adds 50 new issues monthly to prevent contamination.

title: Swarm Pattern: Decentralized Agent Orchestration with Emergent Coordination
description: Decentralized orchestration where agents operate as autonomous peers using shared state (blackboard pattern), local rules, and handoff protocols. Best for brainstorming, research exploration, and fault-tolerant systems. Hardest to debug due to distributed tracing challenges. Requires guardrails to prevent agents spawning excessive subagents and budget limits to control costs.
workflow: Define the shared state/blackboard that all agents can read and write
Create specialist agents with clear role descriptions and local decision rules
Implement handoff protocols: each agent can transfer control to another with context
Set iteration caps and budget limits to prevent runaway agent spawning
Initialize the swarm with the user request on the shared blackboard
Each agent reads the shared state, decides if it can contribute, and acts
Agents write their outputs to the shared state for other agents to build upon
Continue until a termination condition is met (max rounds, consensus, or explicit completion)
Aggregate final state into a coherent output for the user

title: Tool Use: How AI Agents Interact with the Real World
description: In-depth guide on tool use design principles: clear descriptions, constrained parameters, atomic operations, meaningful return values. Covers code execution sandboxing (never run untrusted code directly), multi-tool orchestration class (ToolOrchestrator), parallel tool execution with asyncio.gather, three tool use patterns (RAG, Verification Loop, Progressive Disclosure), and security best practices (allowlists per role, input validation, rate limiting, audit logging).

title: ACE: Agentic Context Evolution — Metacognitive To Retrieve or To Think
description: ACE uses a multi-agent orchestrator with majority voting to dynamically decide between RETRIEVE (external search) and THINK (internal reasoning/sub-query generation) at each step. Achieves 62.8% on HotpotQA (vs. 38.9% for vanilla RAG) using 42% fewer tokens than brute-force IterDRAG, avoiding contextual saturation from over-retrieval.

title: Agentic RAG with Evidence Sufficiency Gating
description: Modern agentic RAG systems dynamically decide when to retrieve, how much to retrieve, and when to stop retrieving by evaluating evidence sufficiency. Instead of single-shot retrieval, the agent iteratively gathers evidence, assesses whether it has enough to answer confidently, and only commits to an answer when the evidence passes a quality threshold.
workflow: RECEIVE: Parse the user query and identify sub-questions that require external evidence
RETRIEVE: For each sub-question, generate targeted search queries and retrieve documents from the knowledge base
ASSESS: Evaluate retrieved evidence for relevance, grounding, and adequacy — use an Evidence Sufficiency Controller or Triple-Check verification
COMMIT or RETRY: If evidence is sufficient, synthesize an answer; if gaps remain, generate new retrieval queries targeting the gaps and loop back
SYNTHESIZE: Merge all sub-answers into a coherent response with citations; flag any remaining uncertainty

title: Anthropic MCP: 10,000+ Community Servers and Registry API for Dynamic Tool Discovery
description: The Model Context Protocol has grown to 10,000+ community-built servers enabling LLMs to connect to external tools, data sources, and APIs. The upcoming Registry API will enable agents to dynamically search for and discover new MCP servers on demand — a key enabler for autonomous tool acquisition. New features include Elicitation (server-initiated user input) and Tasks (durable execution wrappers).

title: AutoGPT Agent Mode: Autonomous Tool Execution Loops in the Platform
description: AutoGPT's SmartDecisionMakerBlock now supports agent_mode_max_iterations for autonomous multi-step tool execution. Setting it to 0 gives traditional single-call mode, 1+ enables agent mode with iteration limits, and -1 enables infinite autonomous mode. Includes proper database operations, Pydantic type safety, and comprehensive tests.

title: AutoGPT Platform: Visual Agent Builder with Autonomous Mode
description: AutoGPT has evolved from a CLI tool into a full agent platform with visual workflow editor, marketplace, and cloud hosting. The SmartDecisionMakerBlock now supports agent_mode_max_iterations for autonomous tool execution loops (0=single-call, N=limited iterations, -1=infinite). Supports OpenAI, Claude, LLaMA, and other LLM providers.

title: BrainMem: Brain-Inspired Evolving Memory for Embodied Agent Task Planning
description: BrainMem implements a three-tier memory hierarchy (working, episodic, semantic) with guided-retry for failed tasks, utility scoring for semantic guideline pruning, and episode-level consolidation via LLM summarization. Achieves 75.0% success on EB-ALFRED (up from 56.3% without memory), with the largest gains on long-horizon and spatial tasks.

title: Browser Automation Agent with Playwright MCP (TypeScript)
description: An autonomous browser agent that uses Playwright MCP to navigate web pages, fill forms, extract data, and take actions through structured accessibility snapshots rather than screenshots. Demonstrates the observe-think-act loop for web tasks with token-efficient accessibility tree parsing.

title: Claude Code Agent Teams: Multi-Agent Orchestration with Shared Task Lists
description: Agent Teams enable one lead and multiple teammates, each with a full context window. They share a task list with dependency tracking and file locking for race conditions. Teammates communicate via mailboxes (not just through the lead). Token cost is significant since each teammate is a separate Claude instance.

title: Claude Code Parallel Worktree Agent Orchestration
description: Claude Code supports five levels of parallelism: subagents (delegated workers inside one session), agent view (background session dispatch), agent teams (coordinated sessions with shared task lists), worktrees (isolated git checkouts for conflict-free parallel work), and /batch (automated PR generation across worktrees). Worktrees are the key primitive that makes multi-agent file editing safe.
workflow: PLAN: Break the task into independent subtasks that can run in parallel without file-level conflicts
DISPATCH: Launch each subtask as a separate Claude Code session with `claude --worktree <name>`, creating isolated git checkouts under `.claude/worktrees/`
EXECUTE: Each worktree session independently reads, edits, tests, and commits changes on its own branch — no merge conflicts during execution
REVIEW: Use `claude agents` to monitor all background sessions; review each worktree's changes before merging
MERGE: Merge completed worktree branches back into the main branch; auto-cleanup removes empty worktrees, dirty ones prompt keep-or-discard

title: CraniMem: Cranial-Inspired Gated and Bounded Memory for Agents
description: CraniMem implements a dual-store architecture (bounded episodic buffer + knowledge graph) with goal-conditioned gating and consolidation loops. Its noise-robustness is best-in-class: only 0.011 F1 drop under noise injection (vs. 0.027 for vanilla RAG). Goal-directed gating prevents distractors from entering memory.

title: CrewAI Autonomous Research Crew (Python)
description: A CrewAI example defining a multi-agent crew with role-based agents, sequential task delegation, and tool integration. Each agent has a specialized role, goal, and backstory, and tasks are executed in order with context passing between them.
code:
from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process
from crewai_tools import SerperDevTool

search_tool = SerperDevTool()

researcher = Agent(
    role="Senior Research Analyst",
    goal="Discover and analyze cutting-edge AI research",
    backstory="You are an experienced researcher who finds the most relevant and recent information.",
    tools=[search_tool],
    verbose=True,
    max_iter=5,
    respect_context_window=True,
)

writer = Agent(
    role="Tech Content Writer",
    goal="Transform research into clear, engaging articles",
    backstory="You are a skilled writer who makes complex topics accessible.",
    verbose=True,
    max_iter=3,
)

research_task = Task(
    description="Research the latest developments in autonomous AI agents.",
    expected_output="A structured summary with 5 key findings and sources.",
    agent=researcher,
)

write_task = Task(
    description="Write a compelling article based on the research findings.",
    expected_output="A 500-word article with introduction, key points, and conclusion.",
    agent=writer,
)

crew = Crew(
    agents=[researcher, writer],
    tasks=[research_task, write_task],
    process=Process.sequential,
    verbose=True,
)

result = crew.kickoff()
print(result)

title: DualRAG: Dual-Process Reasoning and Retrieval Integration

Approaches oracle-knowledge performance on HotpotQA and 2wikiMultiHopQA while using only 1.6-2.2 retrieval iterations vs. IRCoT's 3.7-4.3.

title: Evaluator-Optimizer Pattern: Generate-Evaluate-Loop for Autonomous Agents
description: The evaluator-optimizer pattern uses one LLM to generate content and another to evaluate it against quality criteria. The loop continues until the evaluator certifies the output meets the threshold. This is the canonical pattern for self-correcting agents in LangGraph, where a structured output validator serves as the evaluator and the generator is the optimizer.

title: HASHIRU: Hierarchical Agent System for Hybrid Intelligent Resource Utilization
description: HASHIRU implements a CEO-Employee hierarchy with an economic model (hiring cost + invocation cost + expense cost) for explicit budget management. Prioritizes smaller local LLMs (3B-7B via Ollama) while flexibly using external APIs for complex tasks. Demonstrates autonomous budget refusal when allocation is exceeded.

title: HECG Hierarchical Error-Correcting Agent Loop for Embodied Tasks
description: HECG reformulates plan execution as a graph-based, feedback-driven process with three hierarchical correction levels: L1 (local parameter adjustment), L2 (option switching to an alternative action), and L3 (full task re-planning preserving failure history). A fourth level escalates to human-in-the-loop for safety-critical situations.
workflow: PLAN: Generate an action sequence using an LLM planner with error history context
EXECUTE: Execute the next action in the sequence within a simulated or physical environment
L1-CORRECT: If a small continuous error is detected (e.g., misaligned gripper), adjust parameters and retry the same action
L2-SWITCH: If L1 corrections fail repeatedly, select an alternative action from the candidate set (e.g., push instead of grasp)
L3-REPLAN: If L2 also fails or a cascading error occurs, regenerate the entire remaining action sequence, preserving failure history for context
L4-ESCALATE: If the task is safety-critical or all correction levels fail, pause execution and escalate to a human operator

title: Hierarchical Memory Theory for Language Agents (CoALA Framework)
description: A unifying formal theory for hierarchical memory systems using three operators: Extraction (raw data to atomic units), Coarsening (partitioning with representatives), and Traversal (query-directed retrieval). Introduces the self-sufficiency spectrum and Coarsening-Traversal coupling principle, mapping 11 existing systems including RAPTOR, GraphRAG, xMemory, and H-MEM.

title: Kumiho: Graph-Native Cognitive Memory with AGM Belief Revision
description: Kumiho implements a property graph memory architecture with AGM belief revision semantics, supporting working, episodic, semantic, procedural, and associative memory types. from crewai import Agent, Task, Crew, Process

title: PIVOT: Plan-Inspect-eVOlve Trajectory Optimization for Self-Correcting Agents
description: PIVOT treats agent trajectories as optimizable objects, using natural-language feedback as surrogate textual gradients. It identifies the earliest divergence between planned and executed trajectories, repairs only the unsupported suffix (local repair, not full regeneration), and applies a monotonic acceptance rule so solution quality never degrades across iterations.
workflow: PLAN: Generate one or more candidate action trajectories for the task
INSPECT: Execute each trajectory in a sandbox and compute structured textual gradients encoding plan-vs-execution discrepancies
EVOLVE: Using the discrepancy signals, repair only the unsupported suffix after the earliest divergence point — never regenerate the full plan
VERIFY: Run a final global constraint check on the evolved trajectory; accept only if it improves over the previous best (monotonic acceptance)
RETRY: If verification fails or constraints are unsatisfied, loop back to EVOLVE with the updated gradient signals; exit after max iterations or acceptance

title: Planner-Centric DAG Framework: Decoupling Planning from Execution
description: Decouples planning from execution by having a dedicated Planner model generate a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for the execution plan, enabling parallel tool execution. Uses hierarchical rewards (GRPO) that penalize structural errors and reward edge F1. A Qwen3-8B planner achieves 59.8% SoPR on StableToolBench, outperforming GPT-4 ReAct at 48.2%, with only 2.29 average inference steps.

title: Pre-Act: Multi-Step Planning Before Action Outperforms ReAct
description: Pre-Act extends ReAct by generating a complete multi-step execution plan with detailed reasoning before acting, then incrementally refining the plan after each step. Outperforms ReAct by 70% in action recall on Almita. A fine-tuned Llama 3.1 70B model outperforms GPT-4, showing smaller models can match larger ones with proper training.

title: PreFlect: From Retrospective to Prospective Reflection in LLM Agents
description: PreFlect shifts agent reflection from post-hoc correction to pre-execution foresight. By distilling recurring failure patterns from historical trajectories and critiquing plans before execution, it achieves 59.39% on GAIA with Gemini-2.5-pro, outperforming more complex multi-agent architectures by 3%+.

title: Production Agent Monitoring and Graceful Degradation Pipeline
description: A production-ready pipeline for autonomous agents that monitors decision quality (not just uptime), enforces per-agent token budgets, implements model-fallback chains, and provides kill switches for runaway agents. Based on SRE practices adapted for AI agent systems, including judgment SLOs and behavioral chaos testing.
workflow: INSTRUMENT: Emit OpenTelemetry spans for every agent decision, tool call, and token spend; define judgment SLOs (decision quality, not just latency/uptime)
BUDGET: Set per-agent and per-operation-class token budgets; track token_cost_per_task as a leading indicator — a 40% spike precedes visible quality degradation by 24-48 hours

DEGRADE: Implement a 4-level fallback chain: (1) retry with exponential backoff, (2) model fallback (Opus -> Sonnet -> Haiku), (3) queue and defer non-urgent tasks, (4) graceful decline with user notification
RESPOND: When an alert fires (cost spike, quality drop, loop detection), execute the runbook: kill the runaway agent, fall back to cheaper model, notify the oncall, and preserve forensic traces
LEARN: Run weekly behavioral chaos tests (inject malformed tool responses, contradictory instructions) to surface failure modes before they hit production

title: ReAct Agent Loop: Reason-Act-Observe for Autonomous Tool-Using Agents
description: The ReAct pattern interleaves reasoning (thought) and acting (tool calls) in an observe-think-act loop. The agent generates a thought tracing its reasoning, selects and invokes a tool, observes the result, then decides whether to continue or return a final answer. This is the foundational pattern used by LangGraph, OpenHands, and most modern agent frameworks.
workflow: RECEIVE: Parse the user query into the agent's initial state
THINK: The LLM generates a reasoning trace (thought) about what to do next
ACT: If the thought indicates a tool call, invoke the specified tool with the given arguments and capture the observation
OBSERVE: Append the tool result (or direct LLM response) to the message history
DECIDE: If the last message contains tool calls, route back to ACT; if it contains a final answer, route to END; enforce a recursion_limit to prevent infinite loops

title: Self-Debugging Agent with OpenHands SDK (Python)
description: Using the OpenHands SDK V1 to create a self-debugging code agent that writes code, runs tests, observes failures, and iteratively corrects its own output. Demonstrates the action-execution-observation loop with event-sourced state management.
code:
from openhands.sdk import Agent, ConversationState, LLM, Tool
from openhands.sdk.tools import BashTool, FileEditTool

llm = LLM(model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514")
tools = [BashTool(), FileEditTool()]

agent = Agent(
    name="self_debugging_coder",
    llm=llm,
    tools=tools,
    instructions="""You are a coding agent. Write code, run tests, and fix failures iteratively.
    1. Read the problem description.
    2. Write initial implementation.
    3. Run the provided tests.
    4. If tests fail, read the error output and fix the code.
    5. Repeat until all tests pass or max 3 iterations.""",
)

state = ConversationState()
state.add_user_message("Write a Python function that finds the longest palindromic substring. Include tests.")

for i in range(5):  # max iterations
    action = agent.get_next_action(state)
    if action is None:
        break
    observation = action.execute(state)
    state.add_observation(observation)
    if state.has_final_answer():
        break

print(state.final_answer)

title: 4-Phase Claude Code Workflow (Plan then Implement then Verify)
description: Boris Cherny recommended workflow: Explore (Plan Mode, no changes) then Plan (Plan Mode, review with Ctrl+G) then Implement (Normal/Auto) then Commit (with descriptive message).
workflow: 1. EXPLORE: Enter Plan Mode (Ctrl+G), read files, understand architecture, ask clarifying questions. No changes.
2. PLAN: Still in Plan Mode, create detailed implementation plan. Review with Ctrl+G. Get user approval.
3. IMPLEMENT: Exit Plan Mode. Execute the approved plan step by step. Run tests after each step.
4. VERIFY: Run full test suite. Take screenshots for UI changes. Verify with expected outputs.
5. COMMIT: git add specific files, git commit with descriptive message. Create PR if needed.
6. CLEAN UP: /clear between unrelated tasks. /compact at ~75% context usage.

title: Error Recovery and Debugging Pattern
description: How to recover from common Claude Code errors and debug failing sessions.
workflow: 1. Read the error message carefully. Claude Code usually includes the file path and line number.
2. If Edit fails: check that old_string matches exactly (including whitespace). Use Read first to verify.
3. If Bash fails: check the command syntax. Try running it manually first.
4. If context window is full: run /compact to summarize, then /clear if needed.
5. If MCP server fails: check server logs, restart with docker compose restart <service>.
6. If tool call is denied: check permissions in settings.json or CLAUDE.md.
7. For persistent issues: start a new session with /clear and re-approach the problem.
8. Escalate: use gh issue create to file a bug report with reproduction steps.

title: A/B Testing Fine-Tuned Models in Production
description: Production A/B testing validates LLM improvements with real-world metrics. Meta's RLPF study achieved 6.7% CTR increase (p=0.0296) in a 10-week A/B test with 35K advertisers. Key practices: (1) Use log-binomial regression with covariates for statistical rigor, (2) Offline reward model scores correlate 0.95 Pearson with live A/B outcomes, enabling pre-deployment screening, (3) Watch for reward hacking (model gaming the metric), (4) Use multi-objective optimization (helpfulness + safety + target metric), (5) Moderate optimization levels often outperform aggressive ones.

title: Axolotl: Configurable LLM Fine-Tuning Framework
description: Axolotl is a YAML-driven fine-tuning framework supporting the widest range of models and training methods. Features include DeepSpeed ZeRO 1/2/3 for multi-GPU, SonicMoE/ScatterMoE fused LoRA for MoE models, ND Parallelism (context + tensor + FSDP), SageAttention, GDPO, reward modeling, FP8 fine-tuning via torchao, and multimodal training (vision, audio). Now uses uv-first installation and includes AI agent documentation for Claude Code, Cursor, and Copilot.

title: Continuous Fine-Tuning with Catastrophic Forgetting Mitigation
description: Multi-stage continual fine-tuning requires conflict resolution between new knowledge and existing capabilities. The NAACL 2025 paper introduces preference-based learning bias to identify knowledge conflicts, self-distillation data augmentation for expanding training data, and dynamic data selection using mutual information. After 5 stages of fine-tuning on Llama3-8B, this method achieves 74.8% accuracy vs. 27.7% for traditional continual instruction fine-tuning (46.9% improvement). Key metrics: Knowledge Gain Ratio, post-injection accuracy, and Knowledge Retention Rate.

title: DPO Alignment Fine-Tuning Workflow
description: Step-by-step workflow for aligning an SFT model using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). DPO is simpler and more stable than PPO-based RLHF because it eliminates the need for a separate reward model. The workflow covers on-policy preference data generation, DPO training with TRL's DPOTrainer, and evaluation with reward margins and benchmark scores.
workflow: Start with a fine-tuned SFT model (not a base model)
Generate on-policy preference data: prompt the SFT model, score outputs with a reward function, create (chosen, rejected) pairs
Format preference dataset with 'prompt', 'chosen', and 'rejected' columns
Configure DPOTrainer with beta=0.1 (alignment strength), learning_rate=5e-6 (10-100x smaller than SFT), and QLoRA for memory efficiency
Train with DPOTrainer, monitoring rewards/chosen, rewards/rejected, and reward margins
Evaluate on held-out preference data and general capability benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K)
Watch for reward hacking: model may learn to game the reward signal rather than genuinely improving
Merge the DPO adapter with the SFT model for deployment

title: Dataset Preparation and Formatting for SFT
description: Working Python code for preparing a dataset in the three standard formats (Alpaca instruction, chat messages, and prompt-completion), applying chat templates, splitting into train/validation/test, and pushing to HuggingFace Hub. Demonstrates the most common data preparation patterns used in production fine-tuning pipelines.
code:
from datasets import load_dataset, DatasetDict
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

# Load raw dataset
raw = load_dataset('HuggingFaceH4/no_robots', split='train')
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct')

# Format 1: Alpaca-style instruction format
def format_alpaca(example):
    return {
        'instruction': example['instruction'],
        'input': example.get('input', ''),
        'output': example['response'],
    }

alpaca_data = raw.map(format_alpaca)

# Format 2: Chat/messages format (OpenAI-style)
def format_messages(example):
    messages = []
    if example.get('system_prompt'):
        messages.append({'role': 'system', 'content': example['system_prompt']})
    messages.append({'role': 'user', 'content': example['instruction']})
    messages.append({'role': 'assistant', 'content': example['response']})
    return {
        'messages': messages,
        'text': tokenizer.apply_chat_template(messages, tokenize=False),
    }

chat_data = raw.map(format_messages)

# Format 3: Prompt-completion format
def format_prompt_completion(example):
    prompt = example['instruction']
    if example.get('input'):
        prompt += f'\n\n{example["input"]}'
    return {
        'prompt': prompt,
        'completion': example['response'],
    }

pc_data = raw.map(format_prompt_completion)

# Split into train/val/test (90/5/5)
split = chat_data.train_test_split(test_size=0.1, seed=42)
test_val = split['test'].train_test_split(test_size=0.5, seed=42)
final_dataset = DatasetDict({
    'train': split['train'],
    'validation': test_val['train'],
    'test': test_val['test'],
})

final_dataset.push_to_hub('my-username/my-sft-dataset')
print(f'Train: {len(final_dataset["train"])} samples')
print(f'Val: {len(final_dataset["validation"])} samples')
print(f'Test: {len(final_dataset["test"])} samples')

title: FSDP Multi-GPU Fine-Tuning Workflow
description: Step-by-step workflow for distributed fine-tuning of large models using PyTorch FSDP2 (Fully Sharded Data Parallel). Covers RAM-efficient model loading (rank-0 only), sharded checkpoints, Flash Attention V2, gradient checkpointing, and mixed precision policies. Enables fine-tuning 70B models on as few as 16 A100-80GB GPUs.
workflow: Install dependencies: pip install torch transformers accelerate datasets trl peft
Configure FSDP2 sharding with fully_shard() on transformer blocks + root model
Set MixedPrecisionPolicy: param_dtype=bfloat16, reduce_dtype=float32 for stable gradient reduction
Load model on rank 0 only; use sync_module_states=True for broadcasting to other ranks
Enable Flash Attention V2 (attn_implementation='flash_attention_2') for memory-efficient attention
Enable gradient checkpointing (gradient_checkpointing=True) to reduce peak VRAM
Use SHARDED_STATE_DICT for intermediate checkpoint saves; FULL_STATE_DICT only for final export

title: Fine-Tuning LLMs with LoRA and QLoRA: A Practical Deep-Dive

title: Flash Attention 2 for Memory-Efficient Fine-Tuning
description: Flash Attention 2 is a hardware-aware attention algorithm that reduces memory from O(N^2) to O(N) and achieves 2-4x wall-clock speedup over standard PyTorch attention. Essential for fine-tuning models with long context windows (4K-128K tokens). Integration requires attn_implementation='flash_attention_2' in from_pretrained(), torch >=2.0, and flash-attn package. Reduces peak VRAM by 30-50% for 8K+ sequence lengths, enabling larger batch sizes and longer sequences on the same GPU.

title: GRPO Fine-Tuning Workflow (DeepSeek-R1 Style)
description: Step-by-step workflow for fine-tuning with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), the algorithm behind DeepSeek-R1's reasoning capabilities. GRPO eliminates the need for a critic model by using group-relative rewards, reducing VRAM by ~25% vs. PPO. Each prompt generates a group of completions, ranked against each other, and the model learns to prefer higher-ranked outputs.
workflow: Start with a strong SFT model (e.g., Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct or DeepSeek-R1-Distill)
Define a rule-based or model-based reward function for your task (e.g., format compliance, answer correctness, reasoning quality)
Configure GRPOTrainer with num_generations=8-16 (group size per prompt), beta=0.04 (KL penalty), learning_rate=1e-6
For each prompt, generate num_generations completions, score each with the reward function, and compute group-relative advantages
Train with GRPOTrainer, monitoring reward mean, reward std, and KL divergence to reference model
GRPO uses ~25% less VRAM than PPO because no critic model is needed, but requires more compute per step (multiple generations per prompt)
Evaluate on reasoning benchmarks (GPQA, MATH-500, LiveCodeBench) and general benchmarks (MMLU) to check for alignment tax
Merge the GRPO adapter for deployment; the model should now show improved chain-of-thought reasoning

Includes KL divergence deep dive and practical hyperparameter guidance.

title: How to Create Data for Fine-Tuning LLMs (DigitalOcean)

title: HuggingFace AutoTrain: No-Code LLM Fine-Tuning
description: HuggingFace AutoTrain provides a web UI and CLI for fine-tuning LLMs without writing code. Supports SFT, DPO, and reward model training. Upload a CSV/JSONL dataset, select a model, configure hyperparameters (or use defaults), and launch training on free T4 GPUs or paid A100s. Outputs a merged model ready for deployment. Best for rapid prototyping and teams without ML engineering resources.

title: HuggingFace PEFT: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Library
description: The foundational library for parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods including LoRA, QLoRA, AdaLoRA, IA3, prefix tuning, prompt tuning, and DoRA. Provides get_peft_model() for wrapping any HuggingFace model, merge_and_unload() for adapter merging, add_weighted_adapter() for multi-adapter composition, and push_to_hub() for sharing adapters. The standard integration point for Unsloth, Axolotl, and torchtune.

title: Knowledge Distillation as Fine-Tuning Pretraining
description: Use knowledge distillation from a larger teacher model to generate high-quality training data for a smaller student model. The teacher generates completions for your prompts, which become the student's SFT targets. This produces domain-aligned data with teacher-level reasoning quality, at a fraction of the teacher's inference cost after distillation. Particularly effective for creating reasoning-focused SFT data when GPT-4/Claude-level outputs are needed but inference cost is a concern at scale.

title: LLM Fine-Tuning Dataset Preparation: End-to-End Guide
description: Comprehensive guide covering SFT, DPO/reward model, and preference learning data formats with detailed schemas. Includes deduplication (exact + MinHash/SimHash), PII redaction, safety taxonomy, split leakage prevention, and a QA checklist. Recommends 80k SFT items as baseline: 60% general instructions, 25% domain tasks, 15% policy/safety.

title: LoRA Adapter Merging and Export to GGUF
description: Working Python code for merging a trained LoRA adapter back into the base model, saving the merged model in fp16 safetensors format, converting to GGUF using llama.cpp, and quantizing to Q4_K_M for efficient local deployment. This is the standard deployment pipeline for LoRA-fine-tuned models destined for Ollama or llama.cpp inference.
code:
from peft import PeftModel
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
import subprocess
import os

base_model_id = 'meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct'
adapter_path = './qlora-adapter'
merged_path = './merged-model'
gguf_path = './llama-3.1-8b-finetuned'

# Step 1: Load base model and merge adapter
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(base_model_id)
base_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    base_model_id,
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    device_map='cpu',  # merge on CPU to avoid VRAM issues
)
model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, adapter_path)
model = model.merge_and_unload()

# Step 2: Save merged model in safetensors format
model.save_pretrained(merged_path, safe_serialization=True)
tokenizer.save_pretrained(merged_path)
print(f'Merged model saved to {merged_path}')

# Step 3: Convert to GGUF F16 format
subprocess.run([
    'python', 'llama.cpp/convert_hf_to_gguf.py',
    merged_path,
    '--outfile', f'{gguf_path}-f16.gguf',
    '--outtype', 'f16',
], check=True)

# Step 4: Quantize to Q4_K_M for deployment
subprocess.run([
    './llama.cpp/llama-quantize',
    f'{gguf_path}-f16.gguf',
    f'{gguf_path}-Q4_K_M.gguf',
    'Q4_K_M',
], check=True)

print(f'GGUF model ready: {gguf_path}-Q4_K_M.gguf')
print('Deploy with: ollama create my-model -f Modelfile')

title: LoRA Adapter Merging and GGUF Export for Local Deployment
description: Complete workflow for merging LoRA adapters into the base model, converting to GGUF format, quantizing for efficient local inference, and deploying with Ollama. Covers the critical step of merging before quantization (never quantize before merging), importance matrix quantization for better quality, and Ollama Modelfile creation.
workflow: Train LoRA adapter and save with trainer.save_model()
Load base model in float16 on CPU: AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(base_id, torch_dtype=float16, device_map='cpu')
Merge adapter with PeftModel.from_pretrained(base_model, adapter_path).merge_and_unload()
Save merged model as safetensors: merged_model.save_pretrained(output_path, safe_serialization=True)
Convert to GGUF F16: python llama.cpp/convert_hf_to_gguf.py ./merged-model --outtype f16
(Optional) Generate importance matrix for better quantization: ./llama-imatrix -m model-f16.gguf -f calibration.txt -o imatrix.dat
Quantize to target format: ./llama-quantize [--imatrix imatrix.dat] model-f16.gguf model-Q4_K_M.gguf Q4_K_M
Create Ollama Modelfile: FROM ./model-Q4_K_M.gguf with system prompt and parameters
Build and test: ollama create my-model -f Modelfile && ollama run my-model
Never re-quantize from an already quantized model; always start from F16/F32

title: LoRA and QLoRA Fine-Tuning Guide 2026 (AI Workflow Lab)
description: A production-oriented 2026 guide covering when fine-tuning beats prompting, LoRA and QLoRA implementation with Llama 3.1-8B-Instruct, evaluation framework with automated pass-rate testing, merging and GGUF conversion for Ollama deployment, DoRA (rank-stabilized LoRA), and hardware requirements table. Includes decision guide for choosing between LoRA, QLoRA, Full FT, and DPO.

title: LoRA+ and QA-LoRA: Improved Fine-Tuning Methods
description: LoRA+ improves on standard LoRA by using different learning rates for the A and B matrices (eta_B = 16 * eta_A), achieving 2-5% better downstream performance at the same training cost. QA-LoRA (Quantization-Aware LoRA) introduces group-wise operators that align quantization and LoRA dimensions, eliminating the accuracy degradation that standard QLoRA can cause on large-scale models. Both are available in PEFT via use_dora=True and in Axolotl configs.

title: LoRA/QLoRA Fine-Tuning Pipeline with PEFT
description: Complete step-by-step workflow for fine-tuning an LLM using LoRA or QLoRA with HuggingFace PEFT. Covers configuration of LoraConfig, selecting target modules (all linear layers recommended), training with SFTTrainer, merging adapters back into the base model, and deploying the merged model. This is the foundational workflow that 90% of production fine-tuning jobs follow.
workflow: Install dependencies: pip install transformers peft trl datasets bitsandbytes accelerate
Load a base model and tokenizer with AutoModelForCausalLM and AutoTokenizer
Configure LoRA with LoraConfig (r=16, lora_alpha=32, target_modules=['q_proj','k_proj','v_proj','o_proj','gate_proj','up_proj','down_proj'], task_type='CAUSAL_LM')
For QLoRA: add BitsAndBytesConfig (load_in_4bit=True, bnb_4bit_quant_type='nf4', bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16)
Wrap model with get_peft_model(model, lora_config) and print trainable parameters
Prepare dataset in chat format using tokenizer.apply_chat_template()
Configure SFTConfig with learning_rate=2e-4 (LoRA) or 2e-5 (QLoRA), per_device_train_batch_size=4, gradient_accumulation_steps=4, bf16=True, gradient_checkpointing=True
Train with SFTTrainer and monitor loss, reward margins, and token accuracy
Save adapter with trainer.save_model()
Merge adapter into base model with model.merge_and_unload() for deployment

title: MT-Bench and LLM-as-Judge for Fine-Tuned Model Evaluation
description: Comprehensive guide to evaluating fine-tuned LLMs using MT-Bench (multi-turn benchmark) and LLM-as-Judge methodology. MT-Bench evaluates conversation quality across 8 categories with 80 multi-turn questions. LLM-as-Judge uses GPT-4 or Claude to rate model outputs on a 1-10 scale, achieving 0.85 correlation with human annotators. Covers prompt design for judging, inter-rater agreement metrics, and common evaluation pitfalls like verbosity bias and position bias.

title: ORPO Alignment Fine-Tuning Workflow

title: QLoRA Fine-Tuning with PEFT and BitsAndBytes
description: Working Python code for 4-bit QLoRA fine-tuning of a 7B parameter model on a single consumer GPU. Demonstrates the BitsAndBytes quantization config, LoRA adapter setup with all linear projections as targets, and training with SFTTrainer. The 4-bit quantization reduces VRAM from ~16GB (LoRA) to ~6GB (QLoRA) for 7B models, enabling fine-tuning on free Colab T4 GPUs.
code:
import torch
from datasets import load_dataset
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, BitsAndBytesConfig, TrainingArguments
from trl import SFTTrainer, SFTConfig

model_id = 'meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct'

# 4-bit quantization config
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type='nf4',
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
)

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_id,
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map='auto',
    attn_implementation='flash_attention_2',
)
model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model)

# LoRA config targeting all linear projections
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16,
    lora_alpha=32,
    target_modules=['q_proj', 'k_proj', 'v_proj', 'o_proj', 'gate_proj', 'up_proj', 'down_proj'],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias='none',
    task_type='CAUSAL_LM',
)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()

# Load and format dataset
dataset = load_dataset('trl-lib/Capybara', split='train')

tokenizer.chat_template = None  # use model's default

targs = SFTConfig(
    output_dir='./qlora-output',
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=2,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
    learning_rate=2e-5,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=10,
    gradient_checkpointing=True,
    optim='paged_adamw_8bit',
    save_strategy='epoch',
    max_seq_length=2048,
)

trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model,
    args=targs,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    processing_class=tokenizer,
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model('./qlora-adapter')

title: DDP with Gradient Accumulation and Mixed Precision
description: Production-ready DDP training script with gradient accumulation and mixed precision.
code:
import torch
import torch.distributed as dist
from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
from torch.cuda.amp import autocast, GradScaler

def train():
    dist.init_process_group(backend='nccl')
    local_rank = int(os.environ['LOCAL_RANK'])
    torch.cuda.set_device(local_rank)
    model = DDP(MyModel().cuda(local_rank), device_ids=[local_rank])
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)
    scaler = GradScaler()
    accum_steps = 4  # effective batch = batch * accum * world_size
    for step, (data, target) in enumerate(train_loader):
        data, target = data.cuda(local_rank), target.cuda(local_rank)
        with autocast():
            loss = model(data, target) / accum_steps
        scaler.scale(loss).backward()
        if (step + 1) % accum_steps == 0:
            scaler.step(optimizer)
            scaler.update()
            optimizer.zero_grad()

import torch
import gc

def print_gpu_memory():
    """Print current GPU memory usage."""
    allocated = torch.cuda.memory_allocated() / 1024**3
    reserved = torch.cuda.memory_reserved() / 1024**3
    max_allocated = torch.cuda.max_memory_allocated() / 1024**3
    print(f"Allocated: {allocated:.2f} GB, Reserved: {reserved:.2f} GB, Peak: {max_allocated:.2f} GB")

def free_gpu_memory():
    """Force release unused GPU memory."""
    gc.collect()
    torch.cuda.empty_cache()
    torch.cuda.reset_peak_memory_stats()

# Prevent OOM with gradient checkpointing

class CheckpointedModel(torch.nn.Module):
    def forward(self, x):
        # Use gradient checkpointing to trade compute for memory
        return checkpoint(self.expensive_layer, x)

def setup_distributed():
    dist.init_process_group(backend="nccl")
    local_rank = int(os.environ["LOCAL_RANK"])
    torch.cuda.set_device(local_rank)
    return local_rank

def main():
    local_rank = setup_distributed()
    model = MyModel().cuda(local_rank)
    model = DDP(model, device_ids=[local_rank])
    optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3 * dist.get_world_size())

        sampler.set_epoch(epoch)

            loss = model(batch.cuda(local_rank))
            loss.backward()
            optimizer.step()

import torch
from torch.cuda.amp import autocast, GradScaler

model = MyModel().cuda()
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
scaler = GradScaler()  # Handles FP16 gradient scaling

for epoch in range(num_epochs):
    for batch_idx, (data, target) in enumerate(train_loader):
        data, target = data.cuda(), target.cuda()
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        
        with autocast():  # Enable mixed precision
            output = model(data)
            loss = criterion(output, target)
        
        scaler.scale(loss).backward()  # Scale gradients
        scaler.step(optimizer)  # Update weights
        scaler.update()  # Update scale factor

# Modelfile for a coding assistant
FROM llama3.2:3b

# System prompt
SYSTEM """You are an expert coding assistant. Follow these rules:
1. Always provide working code examples
2. Explain your reasoning step by step
3. Use best practices and modern patterns
4. Include error handling
5. Prefer type hints in Python
"""

# Parameters
PARAMETER temperature 0.7
PARAMETER top_p 0.9
PARAMETER num_ctx 8192
PARAMETER stop "<|eot_id|>"
PARAMETER stop "<|end_of_text|>"

# Template
TEMPLATE """{{- if .System }}<|start_header_id|>system<|end_header_id|>{{ .System }}<|eot_id|>{{- end }}
<|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>{{ .Prompt }}<|eot_id|>
<|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>"""

description: Python script launching SGLang server with RadixAttention and multi-modal support.
code:
import sglang as sgl

# Launch SGLang server with RadixAttention
server = sgl.Runtime(
    model_path="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct",
    tp_size=4,                # 4-way tensor parallelism
    mem_fraction_static=0.85, # 85% GPU memory for KV cache
    chunked_prefill_size=8192, # Optimal prefill chunk size
    enable_overlap_schedule=True, # Overlap prefill and decode
)

# Multi-turn conversation with prefix caching
@sgl.function
def multi_turn_chat(s, question: str, context: str):
    s += sgl.system("You are a helpful assistant.")
    s += sgl.user(context + " " + question)
    s += sgl.assistant(sgl.gen("answer", max_tokens=512))

# Run
states = multi_turn_chat.run_batch(
    [
        {"question": "What is RAG?", "context": "Retrieval-Augmented Generation"},
        {"question": "Explain LoRA", "context": "Low-Rank Adaptation"},
    ],
    temperature=0.7
)

code:
from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams
from vllm.lora.request import LoRARequest

# Launch with LoRA support
llm = LLM(
    model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    enable_lora=True,
    max_loras=4,           # Max concurrent LoRA adapters
    max_lora_rank=16,      # Max LoRA rank
    max_model_len=8192,
    gpu_memory_utilization=0.9,
)

# Define LoRA adapters
lora_requests = [
    LoRARequest("coder", lora_int_id=1, lora_path="./loras/coder-lora"),
    LoRARequest("writer", lora_int_id=2, lora_path="./loras/writer-lora"),
    LoRARequest("analyst", lora_int_id=3, lora_path="./loras/analyst-lora"),
]

# Generate with specific LoRA
outputs = llm.generate(
    prompts=["Write a Python function to sort a list"],
    sampling_params=SamplingParams(temperature=0.7, max_tokens=256),
    lora_request=lora_requests[0],  # Use coder LoRA
)

import asyncio
from mcp import ClientSession, StdioServerParameters
from mcp.client.stdio import stdio_client

async def main():
    server_params = StdioServerParameters(
        command="python",
        args=["server.py"]
    )

    async with stdio_client(server_params) as (read, write):
        async with ClientSession(read, write) as session:
            await session.initialize()

            # List available tools
            tools_result = await session.list_tools()
            for tool in tools_result.tools:
                print(f"Tool: {tool.name} - {tool.description}")

            # Call a tool
            result = await session.call_tool("add", arguments={"a": 5, "b": 3})
            print(f"Result: {result.content[0].text}")

asyncio.run(main())

title: A/B Testing with Consistent Hash-Based Routing
description: Python implementation of hash-based A/B traffic splitting for ML models with deterministic user assignment and statistical significance testing.
code:
import hashlib
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

def get_variant(user_id: str, variants: dict[str, float]) -> str:
    """Deterministic hash-based variant assignment."""
    hash_val = int(hashlib.md5(user_id.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
    bucket = (hash_val % 10000) / 10000.0
    cumulative = 0.0
    for variant, weight in variants.items():
        cumulative += weight
        if bucket < cumulative:
            return variant
    return list(variants.keys())[-1]

def analyze_ab_test(
    control: np.ndarray, treatment: np.ndarray, alpha: float = 0.05
) -> dict:
    """Welch's t-test for A/B test significance."""
    t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(control, treatment, equal_var=False)
    ci = stats.t.interval(
        0.95,
        df=len(control) + len(treatment) - 2,
        loc=np.mean(treatment) - np.mean(control),
        scale=stats.sem(treatment - control)
    )
    return {
        "significant": p_value < alpha,
        "p_value": p_value,
        "effect_size": np.mean(treatment) - np.mean(control),
        "confidence_interval": ci
    }

from evidently import ColumnMapping
from evidently.report import Report
from evidently.metric_preset import DataDriftPreset, RegressionPreset
import pandas as pd

reference_data = pd.read_parquet("data/train.parquet")
current_data = pd.read_parquet("data/production_2026_05.parquet")

column_mapping = ColumnMapping(
    target="target",
    prediction="prediction",
    numerical_features=["age", "income", "score"],
    categorical_features=["category", "region"]
)

drift_report = Report(metrics=[
    DataDriftPreset(),
    RegressionPreset()
])
drift_report.run(
    reference_data=reference_data,
    current_data=current_data,
    column_mapping=column_mapping
)

drift_result = drift_report.as_dict()
if drift_result["data_drift"]["share"] > 0.3:
    print("WARNING: Data drift detected! Retraining recommended.")
else:
    print("No significant drift detected. Model is healthy.")

title: FastAPI Model Serving with Health Checks and Metrics
description: Production FastAPI server for ML model serving with health checks, Prometheus metrics, and structured response format.
code:
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, generate_latest
import time

app = FastAPI(title="ML Model Server", version="1.0.0")
model = None
model_version = "v1.0.0"

PREDICTION_COUNT = Counter("predictions_total", "Total predictions")
PREDICTION_LATENCY = Histogram("prediction_latency_seconds", "Prediction latency")

class PredictionRequest(BaseModel):
    features: list[float]

class PredictionResponse(BaseModel):
    prediction: float
    probability: float
    model_version: str
    latency_ms: float

@app.on_event("startup")
async def load_model():
    global model
    model = load_model_from_registry()

@app.get("/health")
async def health():
    return {"status": "healthy", "model_version": model_version}

@app.post("/predict", response_model=PredictionResponse)
async def predict(request: PredictionRequest):
    start = time.time()
    result = model.predict([request.features])
    latency = (time.time() - start) * 1000
    PREDICTION_COUNT.inc()
    PREDICTION_LATENCY.observe(latency / 1000)
    return PredictionResponse(
        prediction=float(result[0]),
        probability=float(result[1]),
        model_version=model_version,
        latency_ms=latency
    )

@app.get("/metrics")
async def metrics():
    return Response(generate_latest(), media_type="text/plain")

Covers installation, configuration, indexing pipeline (chunking, entity extraction, relationship extraction, community detection, summarization), and querying with global, local, and DRIFT search methods. Enables answering holistic questions that standard vector RAG cannot.
workflow: Install: pip install graphrag

Run the indexing pipeline: graphrag index (chunks docs, extracts entities/relationships, builds knowledge graph, detects communities, generates summaries)

Query with local search for entity-specific questions: graphrag query --method local 'Who is Scrooge and what are his relationships?'
Query with DRIFT search for multi-level reasoning: graphrag query --method drift 'How does Scrooge's character develop?'
Monitor costs: use gpt-4o-mini for testing, enable caching, use --method fast for cheaper indexing

title: Cascading Retrieval-Reranking Pipeline (SPLADE to Cross-Encoder to LLM)
description: Production retrieval pattern using a three-stage cascading pipeline: SPLADE or BM25 for first-stage candidate generation, cross-encoder (DeBERTa-v3) for efficient reranking of top-k candidates, and GPT-4 for listwise reranking of the final top-10. Achieves nDCG@10 of 63.7 on DL23, outperforming any single reranker. Cross-encoders remain competitive with LLM-based rerankers at far lower cost.

title: CoRAG: Chain-of-Retrieval Augmented Generation
description: Agentic RAG pattern that trains o1-like RAG models to dynamically reformulate queries step-by-step through rejection sampling-generated intermediate retrieval chains. Supports test-time compute scaling via greedy decoding, best-of-N sampling, and tree search strategies. The model learns to decompose complex queries and reformulate queries upon retrieval failure. Achieves greater than 10 points EM improvement over strong baselines on multi-hop QA and new SOTA on the KILT benchmark. Demonstrates log-linear scaling between token consumption and performance.

title: ColBERT Late Interaction Reranking Pattern
description: Pattern for using ColBERT's MaxSim late interaction scoring as a reranking stage after initial candidate retrieval. ColBERT produces multi-vector token-level embeddings and computes fine-grained similarity via maximum similarity operations. Best used as a reranker on top 100-500 candidates from BM25 or dense retrieval, not as a first-stage retriever. Supports memory-mapped indexes for 90% RAM reduction.

title: LlamaIndex Semantic Chunking Pipeline
description: Working code for semantic chunking with LlamaIndex using the SemanticSplitterNodeParser. Splits documents adaptively based on embedding similarity between sentences rather than fixed chunk sizes, ensuring each chunk contains semantically related content. Includes parameter tuning for buffer_size and breakpoint_percentile_threshold.
code:
from llama_index.core import SimpleDirectoryReader
from llama_index.core.node_parser import SemanticSplitterNodeParser
from llama_index.embeddings.openai import OpenAIEmbedding

# 1. Load documents
documents = SimpleDirectoryReader("./data").load_data()

# 2. Configure semantic splitter
embed_model = OpenAIEmbedding(model="text-embedding-3-small")
semantic_splitter = SemanticSplitterNodeParser(
    buffer_size=1,                        # Sentences per buffer (1 = most sensitive)
    breakpoint_percentile_threshold=95,   # Higher = fewer, larger chunks
    embed_model=embed_model
)

# 3. Split into semantically coherent nodes
nodes = semantic_splitter.get_nodes_from_documents(documents)

# 4. Inspect chunks
for i, node in enumerate(nodes[:5]):
    print(f"Chunk {i} ({len(node.text)} chars):")
    print(f"  {node.text[:200]}...")
    print()

# 5. Build vector index from semantic nodes
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex
index = VectorStoreIndex(nodes, embed_model=embed_model)

# 6. Query
query_engine = index.as_query_engine()
response = query_engine.query("What are the main themes?")
print(response)
